elements.sent-2411-001: Kleene Algebra with Tests (KAT) provides an elegant algebraic framework for describing non-deterministic finite-state computations.
elements.sent-2411-002: Using a small finite set of non-deterministic programming constructs (sequencing, non-deterministic choice, and iteration) it is able to express all non-deterministic finite state control flow over a finite set of primitives.
elements.sent-2411-003: It is natural to ask whether there exists a similar finite set of constructs that can capture all deterministic computation.
elements.sent-2411-004: We show that this is not the case.
elements.sent-2411-005: More precisely, the deterministic fragment of KAT is not generated by any finite set of regular control flow operations.
elements.sent-2411-006: This generalizes earlier results about the expressivity of the traditional control flow operations, i.e., sequential composition, if-then-else and while.
elements.sent-2411-007: A classic theorem by \citet{BohmJacopini1966 states that the control flow operations of sequential composition, if-then-else and while, in combination with variable assignments of the form $x := c$ and variable equality checks of the form $x = c$ (for $c$ a constant), suffice to specify all types of control flow, including those built using non-local control flow constructs such as goto and break.}
elements.sent-2411-008: More formally, their result can be interpreted to mean that the expressivity of Guarded Kleene Algebra with Tests (GKAT)~\cite{GKAT is strictly contained in the deterministic fragment of Kleene Algebra with Tests (KAT)~\cite{KAT .}}
elements.sent-2411-009: We will show that this remains true when GKAT is extended with finitely many additional regular control flow operations.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-011: We introduce the notion of a (deterministic) regular control flow operation, which generalizes familiar program compositions to include deterministic control flow operations not expressible in GKAT, such as repeat-while-changes, while excluding non-local control flow.
elements.sent-2411-012: We consider extensions of GKAT with finitely many regular control flow operations.
elements.sent-2411-013: Our main result is that, for every finite set $\mathcal{O}$ of such operations, there exists a regular control flow operation that cannot be expressed by compositions of operations in $\mathcal{O}$.
elements.sent-2411-014: We identify an infinite but nontrivial generating set of regular control flow operations.
elements.sent-2411-015: We also show that deciding whether a control flow operation is deterministic is coNP-complete.
elements.sent-2411-016: For bounded test alphabets, the problem is solvable in polynomial time.
elements.sent-2411-017: We believe that the results above offer insights into the nature of deterministic control flow, as well as a new style of analysis of interest to programming language researchers.. Specifically:.
elements.sent-2411-018: Prior work on the expressive power of control flow operations~\cite{peterson-kasami-tokura-1973,Kosaraju focuses on known primitives (if-then, while-do, break, goto).}
elements.sent-2411-019: By comparison, our results provide a broader perspective: not only is while-based control flow incomplete, so are all languages consisting of regular control flow operations.
elements.sent-2411-020: This negatively resolves a question of \citet{bogaerts2024preservation , namely whether the deterministic fragment of KAT is generated by finitely many term-definable operations.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Our results demonstrate that KAT and GKAT, with their relatively simple theory, provide a useful vehicle to formalize and answer questions about (deterministic) control flow.
elements.sent-2411-022: We believe our work also provides a good starting point for posing and studying different kinds of (non-local) flow control, such as the continue statement, exceptions, and algebraic effects.
elements.sent-2411-023: KAT and GKAT are useful tools for program analysis and optimization through decidable equational reasoning~\cite{kozen-patron-2000,anderson-foster-guha-etal-2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: However, our understanding of GKAT is impeded by the absence of a characterization of its automata~\cite{GKAT,GKATBisim .}
elements.sent-2411-025: Our main result provides a fresh angle on this question.
elements.sent-2411-026: In \Cref{sec:determinism we study determinism properties for KAT expressions.}
elements.sent-2411-027: In \Cref{sec:main we prove our main result, i.e., item (2) above.}
elements.sent-2411-028: In \Cref{sec:further we discuss some variations of our setting, and related and future work in \Cref{sec:discussion .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: In this section, we attempt to give an overview of our main thesis, and build intuition for its interpretation along the way.
elements.sent-2411-030: We start by looking at (the skeleton of) a very basic programming language, and work towards a semi-formal statement of our results from there.
elements.sent-2411-031: Guarded Kleene Algebra with Tests, or GKAT for short~\cite{KozenTseng,GKAT , is an abstraction of a while-like programming language, where primitive actions are given by a set $\Sigma$, and primitive tests by a set $T$.}
elements.sent-2411-032: Concretely, the set of GKAT programs $\BA$ is generated by: \begin{equation}\BA \ni b, c ::= false \mid true \mid t \in T \mid b\ or\ c \mid b\ and\ c \mid not\ b \tag{tests} \GKAT \ni e, f ::= assert\ b \mid p \in \Sigma \mid e; f \mid \Ifthenelse{b}{e}{f} \mid \while{b}{e} \tag{programs}\end{equation} If we want to give a denotational semantics to such programs, we need to say how the semantics of a composed program arises from its components.
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}\BA \ni b, c ::= false \mid true \mid t \in T \mid b\ or\ c \mid b\ and\ c \mid not\ b \tag{tests} \GKAT \ni e, f ::= assert\ b \mid p \in \Sigma \mid e; f \mid \Ifthenelse{b}{e}{f} \mid \while{b}{e} \tag{programs}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-033: Let us start at the level of tests.
elements.sent-2411-034: If we model the possible states of our machine with a set $S$, then the natural semantics of a test would be the set of all states in which it is true.
elements.sent-2411-035: Given a function $\tau: T \to 2^S$ that does this for each primitive test, we can compute such a semantics in a straightforward manner, as follows. \begin{equation}\sem{false}_\tau = \emptyset \sem{t}_\tau = \tau(t) \sem{b\ or\ c}_\tau = \sem{b}_\tau \cup \sem{c}_\tau \sem{true}_\tau = S \sem{not\ b}_\tau = S \setminus \sem{b}_\tau \sem{b\ and\ c}_\tau = \sem{b}_\tau \cap \sem{c}_\tau\end{equation} Defining the semantics of GKAT programs proper is somewhat less straightforward, but still doable.

elements.sent-2411-036: A natural way to go about it is to model program semantics in terms of a function $f$ on machine states, which represents the effected computation: if the machine is in state $s$ when the program starts, it terminates in state $f(s)$ when the program halts.
elements.sent-2411-037: This model is usually extended to partial functions, where $f(s)$ being undefined is interpreted to mean that the program did not terminate successfully --- either by getting stuck in a loop, or by running into an error.
elements.sent-2411-038: Suppose that for each primitive action $p \in \Sigma$ we have a partial function $\sigma(p): S \rightharpoonup S$ that tells us whether (and how) $p$ affects the machine's state, or fails.
elements.sent-2411-039: We can then give a semantics of programs in terms of partial functions parameterized by $\tau$ and $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-040: The semantics of an assertion just checks whether the current test satisfies the state; the semantics of a primitive action refers to $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Sequential composition is achieved by composition of partial functions.
elements.sent-2411-042: Given this intuition, we can then define the semantics as the smallest partial function that satisfies the following recursive equation: \begin{mathpar \sem{\while{b {e _\tau^\sigma(s) = \begin{cases \sem{\while{b {e _\tau^\sigma(\sem{e _\tau^\sigma(s)) s \in \sem{b _\tau s s \not\in \sem{b _\tau \end{cases \end{mathpar.}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: Formally, $\sem{\while{b {e _\tau^\sigma$ is the least fixed point of the function that sends a partial function $f: S \rightharpoonup S$ to the partial function $f': S \rightharpoonup S$ such that $f'(s) = f(\sem{e _\tau^\sigma(s))$ when $s \in \sem{b _\tau$, and $f'(s) = s$ otherwise.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: This is a Scott-continuous function on the directed-complete partial order on partial functions on $S$ where $f \leq g$ when for all $s \in S$ we have that $f(s) = g(s)$ whenever $f(s)$ is defined, and therefore the least fixed point exists uniquely by Kleene's fixpoint theorem.
elements.sent-2411-045: So far, GKAT and its denotational semantics have been fairly straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-046: The only element that is somewhat non-standard is that we have not fixed a set of primitive tests and actions.
elements.sent-2411-047: Instead, we have parametrized the semantics in terms of the interpretation of these primitives on some abstract set of machine states $S$.
elements.sent-2411-048: Our language is thus purely about how the truth or falsehood of the tests determine which actions are executed, and in which order --- i.e., control flow.
elements.sent-2411-049: At this point, we could choose to fix a set of machine states, introduce common programming language concepts like variables and I/O, and start studying the properties of the resulting language.
elements.sent-2411-050: But instead, let us take advantage of this abstraction, and use GKAT as a vehicle for studying control flow.
elements.sent-2411-051: If we adopt this point of view, then the semantics outlined above is best described as a dependently typed function, whose signature could be written as follows: \begin{equation}\sem{-}: \GKAT \to \forall S : Set, (\underbrace{T \to 2^S}_{test interp. $\tau$}) \to (\underbrace{\Sigma \to (S \rightharpoonup S)}_{action interp. $\sigma$}) \to (S \rightharpoonup S)\end{equation} In other words, $\sem{-}$ is a function that takes an abstract program $e$ from $\GKAT$, and returns for all sets $S$ a partial function $\sem{e _\tau^\sigma$ on $S$, determined by an interpretation of the primitive tests $\tau: T \to 2^S$ and an interpretation of the primitive actions $\sigma: \Sigma \to (S \rightharpoonup S)$.}

elements.sent-2411-061: A natural question to ask, then, is what kind of control flow can be encoded in this language.
elements.sent-2411-062: As a first and --- in our opinion reasonable --- guess, one might propose that this language should be able to express all control flow that is ``programmable''.
elements.sent-2411-063: After all, we have the usual programming constructs at our disposal, so if we know how the primitive tests should affect the actions executed, we should be able to write a program in $\GKAT$ that implements this.
elements.sent-2411-064: Surprisingly, this is not the case: there are types of control flow that could be built with specific primitives, but which cannot be the semantics of an expression in $\GKAT$.
elements.sent-2411-065: The following demonstrates this more formally.

elements.sent-2411-177: We started this section with the goal of formalizing what it meant for control flow to be programmable.
elements.sent-2411-178: To that end, we considered KAT as a generalization of GKAT that includes non-determinism.
elements.sent-2411-179: We have seen how the relational semantics of KAT extends the partial function semantics of GKAT, and how the former can be related to a model based on languages and automata.
elements.sent-2411-180: Furthermore, we have seen that KAT can describe control flow that cannot be expressed in GKAT\@.
elements.sent-2411-181: With these formalisms explained, we can now position KAT as a reasonable model of (non-deterministic) ``programmable'' control flow.
elements.sent-2411-182: Our primary justification for this is the correspondence between the relational model and KAT automata, whose states can be viewed as different ``decision points'' within a program, with the transitions describing which actions are executed, their preconditions, and the resulting control flow.
elements.sent-2411-183: If a program has finitely many decision points (which seems reasonable for a finite program), and the ways of moving between those decision points can be described, it should then be possible to derive a KAT automaton for it, and thus a KAT expression.
elements.sent-2411-184: To further illustrate this point, we note that $F$ can be modeled by the two-state KAT automaton drawn in \Cref{fig:repeat-until-changed-automaton .}
elements.sent-2411-185: The intended meaning of this automaton is somewhat easier to unravel than the equivalent KAT expression encountered earlier: the initialization function checks whether $t$ holds, and ``remembers'' the outcome by transitioning to $q_1$ or $q_2$; the latter two states accept when, upon arrival, the value of $t$ has not changed, and alternate between each other when it has not.
elements.sent-2411-186: We have made the case that KAT is a reasonable model of program flow, expressive to the point of being able to describe all finite shapes of control.
elements.sent-2411-187: The Kleene theorem of KAT, which relates KAT automata to KAT expressions, provides a useful duality: on the one hand, there is the ``flat'' representation in terms of automata, where a program can move between any two states of control, while on the other hand there is the more hierarchical representation in terms of expressions.
elements.sent-2411-188: We have asserted (but not proved) that this operational-denotational correspondence does not translate to GKAT, because $F$ does not correspond to a GKAT program.
elements.sent-2411-189: Nevertheless, non-determinism is not necessary to specify $F$: the KAT automaton in \Cref{fig:repeat-until-changed-automaton is deterministic, in the sense that at most one transition is available in any state at all times; for instance, in $q_1$ the automaton can accept if $t$ holds, or perform $p$ and transition to $q_2$ when it does not.}
elements.sent-2411-190: A reasonable question to ask is whether there is some sort of language that, like GKAT, composes deterministic programs in a deterministic way, and which can express all deterministic control flow described by KAT\@.

elements.sent-2411-549: Our main result can be summarized informally, as follows: compositional control flow languages consisting of a finite set of operators, such as GKAT, are inherently expressively incomplete.
elements.sent-2411-550: Nevertheless, we believe that this result generalizes and adds more depth to earlier insights about control flow.
elements.sent-2411-551: It does not invalidate GKAT as a topic worth studying.
elements.sent-2411-552: On the contrary, we think there are still interesting matters to be settled.
elements.sent-2411-553: If anything, we take this result as encouragement to study the expressiveness of different kinds of control flow primitives, such as exceptions or other limited forms of non-local control flow.
elements.sent-2411-554: We now briefly go over a number of relevant works in this area.
elements.sent-2411-555: Program schematology, i.e., the study of control flow, traces its origins to the aforementioned result by \citet{BohmJacopini1966 about realizing all control flow using composition and iteration.}
elements.sent-2411-556: The transformation from op.\ cit.\ uses a variable to keep track of the current decision point within the program --- not unlike one of the ``workarounds'' we considered to implement the program $F$.
elements.sent-2411-557: The algorithm proposed by \citet{erosa-hendren-1994 , which gradually eliminates non-local control flow from a program, is perhaps more practical; see also~\cite{casse-etal-2002 for an implementation.}}
elements.sent-2411-558: Taking a more systematic approach, \citet{Kosaraju showed that there exists a hierarchy of expressivity of multi-level break statements: the ability to break $n+1$-deeply nested loops affords more expressivity than being able to break $n$-deeply nested loops.}
elements.sent-2411-559: Our result, like Kosaraju's, implies a hierarchy to deterministic control flow constructs.
elements.sent-2411-560: However, we focused on program composition; because non-local control flow (e.g., break) makes sense only in the context of a larger program, it is outside of the scope of our techniques.
elements.sent-2411-561: We are excited about KAT as a lingua franca for rigorously studying expressivity of control flow constructs --- though we are hardly the first to do so~\cite{angus-kozen-2001,KozenTseng,grathwohl-etal-2014,kozen-2008 .}
elements.sent-2411-562: The interested reader is directed to~\cite{zhang-etal-2024 for further research on how to realize non-local control flow within deterministic KAT, and efficiently decide equivalence\@.}
elements.sent-2411-563: A full characterization of the expressivity of this semantics was left open until recently~\cite{grabmayer-2022,grabmayer-fokkink-2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-564: It turns out that the expressivity of a certain fragment of GKAT is intricately related to this work~\cite{kappe-schmid-silva-2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-565: Underlying the efforts in this area is a direct connection of expressivity to completeness of equational reasoning systems for programs; put briefly, if the automata expressed by a certain fragment can be precisely characterized, then a completeness theorem is usually not too far away.
elements.sent-2411-566: In future work, we hope to consider the expressivity problem of the full calculus of GKAT, and by extension its open completeness problem~\cite{GKAT,GKATBisim,kappe-schmid-silva-2023 , using the techniques developed in this article.}

elements.sent-2411-569: Our main theorem then says that there is no finite set of such RPSs into which all other such RPSs can be hierarchically decomposed.
